title: Leveling
description: Differential leveling by the height-of-instrument (HI) method, profile and trigonometric leveling, curvature & refraction, collimation/two-peg testing, and loop misclosure adjustment for vertical control. Covers the field data and computations an FS examinee must perform from benchmarks and datums.
key_topics: Backsight / foresight bookkeeping and the arithmetic check
Trigonometric leveling (Δelev = S·sin V = d·tan V)
Collimation error and the two-peg test
Loop misclosure and adjustment / closure tolerances

content_sections.title: Module Overview
content_sections.content:
## Leveling — the backbone of vertical control

**Leveling** is the field operation that determines **elevations** of points relative to a **datum**. On the FS exam, leveling lives in Knowledge Area 5C (Survey Computations) with strong overlap into Area 1 (field procedures) and Area 7 (error/closure). Expect a handful of direct computational items: read a level note and find an elevation, compute a curvature-and-refraction correction, reduce a trigonometric-leveling sight, or distribute a loop misclosure.

The dominant field technique is **differential leveling** using the **height-of-instrument (HI) method**. A level set up between two points reads a **backsight (BS)** on a point of known elevation (a benchmark or turning point) and a **foresight (FS)** on the next point. Adding the BS to the known elevation gives the line-of-sight elevation, the **HI**; subtracting a FS from the HI gives the new point's elevation. Repeating this from setup to setup carries elevation across long distances.

Three relationships are non-negotiable: **HI = Elev + BS**, **Elev = HI − FS**, and the running check **ΣBS − ΣFS = Elev_last − Elev_first**. Beyond the bookkeeping you must handle systematic effects — **earth curvature and atmospheric refraction**, **instrument collimation** (line-of-sight not level, found by the **two-peg test**) — and quality control through **loop misclosure** against a known datum, then adjusting the observed elevations.

This module also covers **profile leveling** (elevations along a route for cross-sections and grades), **trigonometric leveling** (elevation from a slope distance and vertical angle), **reciprocal leveling** across obstacles, and **three-wire leveling** for higher precision. Datums you will see named: **NAVD88** (the current US vertical datum) and the older **NGVD29**; elevations are **orthometric heights (H)**, related to GPS ellipsoid heights by H = h − N.

content_sections.title: Key Concepts
content_sections.content:
## Datums, benchmarks, and the vertical reference A **datum** is the reference surface from which elevations are measured; in modern US practice that is the **geoid**, realized as **NAVD88**. A **benchmark (BM)** is a permanently monumented point of published, known elevation. A **turning point (TP)** is a temporary, well-defined point (e.g., a turning plate) used to transfer the line of sight from one instrument setup to the next; the rod is held on the same TP for the FS of one setup and the BS of the next. ## Backsight, foresight, and the HI method - **Backsight (BS)** — also called a **plus sight (+)**. A rod reading taken on a point of *known* elevation. **HI = Elev + BS.** - **Foresight (FS)** — also called a **minus sight (−)**. A rod reading on a point whose elevation you are *determining*. **Elev = HI − FS.** - The **HI (height of instrument)** here is the *elevation of the line of sight*, not the height of the telescope above the ground.
StationBS (+)HIFS (−)Elevation
BM-A3.21503.21500.00
TP14.05505.102.16501.05
BM-B1.74503.36
**Arithmetic check:** ΣBS = 3.21 + 4.05 = 7.26; ΣFS = 2.16 + 1.74 = 3.90; ΣBS − ΣFS = 3.36 = Elev_B − Elev_A = 503.36 − 500.00. The check confirms the *bookkeeping* (not blunders in the field). ## Curvature and refraction Over a long sight the earth curves away from the level line of sight (**curvature**, makes rod readings read **too high**), and the line of sight bends downward through the atmosphere (**refraction**, partly offsetting curvature). Net, for rod readings in feet with the sight distance **F expressed in thousands of feet**: - Curvature only: **C = 0.0240·F²** ft - Combined curvature & refraction: **C_R = 0.0206·F²** ft Balancing BS and FS distances **cancels** curvature & refraction in differential leveling — a key reason to keep sight distances equal. ## Collimation error and the two-peg test **Collimation error** is a line of sight that is not truly horizontal when the bubble is centered. The **two-peg test** detects it: set two pegs A and B a known distance apart; from the midpoint, equal sight distances cancel any collimation and give the *true* ΔElev; then set up near one peg (unequal distances) and re-read — the discrepancy is the collimation error per unit of distance difference. In differential leveling the residual collimation effect is **(collimation rate)·(ΣBS dist − ΣFS dist)**, again zeroed by balancing sights. ## Trigonometric leveling When a slope distance **S** (or horizontal distance **d**) and a **vertical angle V** (above horizontal) are measured, the elevation difference is **ΔElev = S·sin V = d·tan V**, adjusted for instrument height (hi) and rod/target height (hr): ΔElev = S·sin V + hi − hr. For long sights add the C_R correction. (Note: if a **zenith angle z** is measured instead, ΔElev = S·cos z.) ## Loop misclosure and adjustment A level **loop** returns to a benchmark of known elevation (or closes back on the start). The **misclosure** = observed (ΣBS − ΣFS) minus the known elevation difference. If within tolerance, distribute the correction in proportion to **distance leveled** (or number of setups). Federal/typical order-of-accuracy tolerances scale with √(distance): e.g., third-order ≈ 0.05·√(miles) ft. **Reciprocal leveling** (reading both ends from each bank) cancels curvature/refraction and collimation across rivers/canyons; **three-wire leveling** averages upper/middle/lower crosshair readings for precision and a built-in check.

content_sections.title: Essential Formulas
content_sections.content:
## Differential leveling (HI method)

- **Height of instrument:** HI = Elev_BM + BS
- **Point elevation:** Elev = HI − FS
- **Net elevation change (multi-setup):** ΔElev = ΣBS − ΣFS
- **Arithmetic (page) check:** Elev_last − Elev_first = ΣBS − ΣFS

## Curvature & refraction (rod readings, USCS)

With sight distance **F in thousands of feet**:

- Curvature only: **C = 0.0240·F²** ft
- Curvature + refraction: **C_R = 0.0206·F²** ft
- (Metric, K in km: C = 0.0785·K² m; C_R = 0.0675·K² m)
- Vertical-angle form (handbook): c ≈ 4.905″/1000 ft; (c & r) ≈ 4.244″/1000 ft

## Trigonometric leveling

- **ΔElev = S·sin(V) = d·tan(V)**  (V = vertical angle above horizontal)
- With instrument/rod heights: **ΔElev = S·sin(V) + hi − hr**
- Zenith-angle form: ΔElev = S·cos(z)
- New elevation: Elev_B = Elev_A + ΔElev

## Loop / line misclosure & adjustment

- **Misclosure** = (ΣBS − ΣFS)_observed − (Elev_known_end − Elev_known_start)
- **Correction at a point** = −Misclosure × (distance to point / total distance)
- Closure tolerance (typical 3rd order): allowable = 0.05·√(M) ft, M = miles leveled

## Reciprocal & three-wire leveling

- **Reciprocal Δh:** Δh = ½[(a₁ − b₁) + (a₂ − b₂)]
- **Three-wire mean reading:** R = (Upper + Middle + Lower)/3; half-interval check: (U − M) ≈ (M − L)
- **Stadia interval → distance:** D ≈ 100·(U − L)

## Collimation

- **Collimation correction:** ΔC = e·(ΣBS dist − ΣFS dist), where e = collimation rate (ft of error per ft of sight, e.g., 0.012/100)

## Orthometric vs ellipsoid height

- **H = h − N** (H orthometric/leveled elevation, h GPS ellipsoid height, N geoid height)

content_sections.title: Worked Examples
content_sections.content:
## Example 1 — HI and a point elevation (HI method) **Given:** BM-A elevation 642.18 ft. The level reads a backsight of **5.42 ft** on BM-A, then a foresight of **7.91 ft** on point P. **Solve:** 1. HI = Elev_BM + BS = 642.18 + 5.42 = **647.60 ft** 2. Elev_P = HI − FS = 647.60 − 7.91 = **639.69 ft** **Calculator (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991):** plain arithmetic — `642.18 + 5.42 =` gives HI, then `Ans − 7.91 =` gives Elev_P. Sanity check: FS > BS, so P is lower than BM-A (639.69 < 642.18). ✓ ## Example 2 — Multi-setup elevation and arithmetic check **Given:** Run from BM-12 (Elev 1004.55 ft) over two turning points to BM-13.
StationBSFS
BM-122.10
TP16.341.55
TP23.028.71
BM-134.18
**Solve:** 1. ΣBS = 2.10 + 6.34 + 3.02 = **11.46**; ΣFS = 1.55 + 8.71 + 4.18 = **14.44** 2. ΔElev = ΣBS − ΣFS = 11.46 − 14.44 = **−2.98 ft** 3. Elev_BM-13 = 1004.55 + (−2.98) = **1001.57 ft** 4. **Arithmetic check:** Elev_last − Elev_first = 1001.57 − 1004.55 = −2.98 = ΣBS − ΣFS ✓ **Calculator:** store ΣBS and ΣFS, subtract, add to start. Keep BS and FS columns aligned so you never mix a plus sight with a minus sight. ## Example 3 — Curvature & refraction **Given:** A single sight of **2,800 ft**. Find the combined curvature-and-refraction correction to the rod reading. **Solve:** F = 2800/1000 = **2.8** (thousands of ft). C_R = 0.0206·F² = 0.0206 × 2.8² = 0.0206 × 7.84 = **0.1615 ft** (read too high; subtract from the rod reading). Curvature alone would be 0.0240 × 7.84 = 0.188 ft; refraction recovers ≈ 0.027 ft. **Calculator:** `2.8 x² × 0.0206 =`. Note balancing BS = FS distances cancels this entirely. ## Example 4 — Trigonometric leveling **Given:** From station A (Elev 500.00 ft) a total station measures to a prism at B: horizontal distance **d = 620.0 ft**, vertical angle **V = +3°45′00″** above horizontal, instrument height hi = 5.30 ft, prism height hr = 5.30 ft (equal). **Solve:** 1. Convert angle: 3°45′ = 3.75°. `3` `°’”` `45` `°’”` `=` on TI-36X gives 3.75°. 2. ΔElev = d·tan(V) = 620.0 × tan(3.75°) = 620.0 × 0.065543 = **40.64 ft** 3. Add hi − hr = 0, so Elev_B = 500.00 + 40.64 = **540.64 ft** **Check with slope form:** S = d/cos V = 620.0/0.99786 = 621.3 ft; ΔElev = S·sin V = 621.3 × 0.065399 = 40.63 ft ✓ (rounding). **Calculator:** put the calculator in DEG mode; for a zenith angle z use `S × cos(z)` instead. ## Example 5 — Loop misclosure & adjustment **Given:** A level loop starts on BM-100 (Elev 250.000 ft) and is run through field points back to **BM-101 (published Elev 263.420 ft)**. The notes give ΣBS = 41.275 ft and ΣFS = 27.810 ft over **0.80 miles**. **Solve:** 1. Observed ΔElev = ΣBS − ΣFS = 41.275 − 27.810 = **13.465 ft** → observed Elev_BM-101 = 250.000 + 13.465 = 263.465 ft 2. Misclosure = observed − known = 263.465 − 263.420 = **+0.045 ft** 3. Allowable (3rd order) = 0.05·√0.80 = 0.05 × 0.894 = 0.045 ft → **just within tolerance** 4. Distribute −0.045 ft across the run in proportion to distance; the corrected closing elevation becomes the published **263.420 ft**. **Calculator:** `0.05 × 0.80 √ =` for the tolerance; sign of the misclosure tells you which way to push the elevations.

content_sections.title: Exam Tips & Calculator Strategy
content_sections.content:
## Strategy for leveling items

- **Memorize the three core relations** so you never look them up: **HI = Elev + BS**, **Elev = HI − FS**, **ΣBS − ΣFS = ΔElev**. These solve the majority of leveling questions in seconds.
- **BS is always a plus, FS is always a minus.** The single most common trap is swapping them. Write a tiny two-column BS/FS table in the margin even for a one-setup problem.
- **Always run the arithmetic check** (ΣBS − ΣFS = last − first). If it fails, you mis-added a column — fix it before trusting the elevation.
- **Watch the units of F in curvature & refraction.** The handbook constants 0.0240 and 0.0206 assume **F in *thousands* of feet**. A 3,000-ft sight is F = 3, not 3000. Forgetting this inflates the answer by 10⁶.
- **Curvature reads the rod too HIGH; refraction lowers the apparent reading.** Net C_R = 0.0206·F² is *subtracted* from a long FS. Balancing BS and FS distances cancels both — a frequent "why do we keep sights equal?" concept question.
- **Trig leveling:** confirm whether the angle is a **vertical angle** (from horizontal → use sin/tan) or a **zenith angle** (from vertical → use cos). Add hi, subtract hr. Put the calculator in **DEG** mode.
- **Misclosure sign:** misclosure = observed − known. Corrections have the *opposite* sign and are spread by **distance or setup count**.

## Approved-calculator tricks (TI-36X Pro / Casio fx-991 / HP 33s-35s)

- **DMS entry:** TI-36X Pro — type the degrees, `°’”`, minutes, `°’”`, seconds, `°’”`, then `=`; Casio fx-991 uses the `°’”` key the same way. This converts 3°45′ to 3.75° for the tan() call. HP 35s: enter `3.4500` then `→HR`.
- **Chaining a level run:** store ΣBS in memory A and ΣFS in memory B as you add the columns, then `RCL A − RCL B` for ΔElev — avoids re-keying.
- **C_R fast path:** `(F) x² × 0.0206 =`. Keep F in thousands of feet.
- **Reciprocal leveling:** compute Δh = ½[(a₁−b₁)+(a₂−b₂)] directly; the averaging cancels curvature/refraction and collimation.
- **Three-wire:** mean = (U+M+L)/3 and check that (U−M) ≈ (M−L); distance ≈ 100·(U−L).

## How the FS Reference Handbook helps

- The handbook's **Curvature and refraction** block gives the 0.0240/0.0206 (ft) and 0.0785/0.0675 (m) constants plus the arc-second forms — search "curvature" rather than re-deriving.
- Leveling bookkeeping (HI, BS, FS) is **not** a boxed formula; you must know it cold. The handbook only supplies the constants and the trig.
- For trig leveling, use the handbook's **trigonometry / right-triangle** relations; for tolerances, leveling closure follows the supplied **FGCS Geodetic Leveling** standard logic (allowable ∝ √distance).

questions.question_text: In differential leveling using the height-of-instrument method, a backsight (BS) is:
options: A rod reading on a point of known elevation, added to that elevation to get the HI
A rod reading on a point of unknown elevation, subtracted from the HI
The horizontal distance from the instrument to the benchmark
The vertical angle measured to a distant target
questions.correct_answer: A rod reading on a point of known elevation, added to that elevation to get the HI
questions.explanation: A backsight (plus sight) is taken on a point of known elevation. HI = Elev + BS. A foresight is the minus sight taken on a point being determined.

questions.question_text: Which equation gives the elevation of a new point from the height of instrument?
questions.explanation: The foresight is subtracted from the line-of-sight elevation (HI): Elev = HI − FS. The backsight is what was added to form the HI.

questions.question_text: BM-A has elevation 318.40 ft. The level reads a backsight of 6.12 ft on BM-A. What is the height of instrument (HI)?
With an HI of 324.52 ft, a foresight of 9.30 ft is read on point P. The elevation of P is:
Over a level run, ΣBS = 18.42 ft and ΣFS = 22.05 ft. The net change in elevation from the first to the last point is:
questions.explanation: ΔElev = ΣBS − ΣFS = 18.42 − 22.05 = −3.63 ft. The endpoint is lower than the start.

questions.question_text: The arithmetic (page) check on a level note verifies that:
options: ΣBS − ΣFS equals the last elevation minus the first elevation
Curvature and refraction have been removed
No field blunders occurred in reading the rod
questions.correct_answer: ΣBS − ΣFS equals the last elevation minus the first elevation
questions.explanation: The check confirms the bookkeeping: ΣBS − ΣFS must equal Elev_last − Elev_first. It does not detect field reading blunders or systematic errors.

questions.question_text: A point used to transfer the line of sight from one instrument setup to the next, on which both a foresight and the following backsight are read, is called a:
questions.explanation: A turning point is a temporary, well-defined point used to carry elevation forward. A benchmark is a permanent point of published elevation.

questions.question_text: For a level sight of 4,000 ft, the combined curvature-and-refraction correction (rod reading, ft) is most nearly:
In differential leveling, the effect of curvature and refraction is essentially eliminated by:
questions.explanation: When BS and FS distances are equal, the curvature-and-refraction (and collimation) errors are the same magnitude on both sights and cancel in the difference.

questions.question_text: Earth curvature causes a level rod reading at a long distance to appear:
questions.explanation: Curvature drops the earth's surface away from the horizontal line of sight, so the line strikes the rod higher, reading too high. Refraction partially offsets this.

questions.question_text: A field test that detects collimation error (a line of sight not truly horizontal) is the:
questions.explanation: The two-peg test compares an equal-distance (cancelling) setup against an unequal-distance setup to isolate the collimation error per unit distance.

questions.question_text: From station A (Elev 600.00 ft) a total station measures a horizontal distance of 500.0 ft to a target at vertical angle +6°00′ (instrument and target heights equal). The elevation of the target is most nearly:
If a total station reads a zenith angle z (measured from the vertical/zenith) and slope distance S, the elevation difference is:
questions.explanation: A zenith angle is measured down from vertical, so the vertical component is S·cos(z). (For a vertical angle V from horizontal, ΔElev = S·sin V.)

questions.question_text: The current official vertical datum for the conterminous United States is:
questions.explanation: NAVD88 is the current US vertical datum (orthometric heights). NGVD29 is its predecessor; NAD83 and WGS84 are horizontal/3-D datums.

questions.question_text: Reciprocal leveling (reading both ends from each bank) is used primarily to:
options: Cancel curvature, refraction, and collimation across a wide obstacle such as a river
Speed up leveling by halving the number of setups
Convert ellipsoid heights to orthometric heights
Eliminate the need for a level rod
questions.correct_answer: Cancel curvature, refraction, and collimation across a wide obstacle such as a river
questions.explanation: Because equal-distance balancing is impossible across a river, reciprocal observations from each bank are averaged: Δh = ½[(a₁−b₁)+(a₂−b₂)], cancelling the systematic errors.

questions.question_text: In three-wire leveling, the rod reading used for the computation is taken as:
options: The mean of the upper, middle, and lower crosshair readings
The difference of the upper and lower readings
questions.correct_answer: The mean of the upper, middle, and lower crosshair readings
questions.explanation: Reading = (U + M + L)/3, with the half-interval check (U − M) ≈ (M − L). The stadia interval also gives the sight distance ≈ 100·(U − L).

questions.question_text: A level loop closes with a misclosure of +0.06 ft over a total distance of 1.5 miles. The allowable third-order closure is 0.05·√(miles). Does the loop meet third-order accuracy?
options: Yes — allowable is 0.061 ft, so 0.06 ft is within tolerance
questions.correct_answer: Yes — allowable is 0.061 ft, so 0.06 ft is within tolerance
questions.explanation: Allowable = 0.05·√1.5 = 0.05 × 1.225 = 0.061 ft. The 0.06 ft misclosure is just within the limit.

questions.question_text: When distributing a level loop misclosure, the correction at each point is proportional to:
options: The distance leveled (or number of setups) to that point
The backsight reading at that point
questions.correct_answer: The distance leveled (or number of setups) to that point
questions.explanation: Random leveling error accumulates with distance, so the correction is distributed in proportion to cumulative distance (or setup count) along the run.

questions.question_text: A backsight of 2.55 ft is read on BM-1 (Elev 410.20 ft) and a foresight of 8.40 ft is read on BM-2. The elevation of BM-2 is:
Orthometric height H (the elevation obtained by leveling) is related to the GPS ellipsoid height h and geoid height N by:
questions.explanation: Leveling yields orthometric heights referenced to the geoid: H = h − N, where h is the ellipsoid height from GPS and N is the geoid undulation.

questions.question_text: Profile leveling is performed primarily to:
options: Determine elevations of points along a route for plotting a profile and designing grades
questions.correct_answer: Determine elevations of points along a route for plotting a profile and designing grades
questions.explanation: Profile leveling collects ground elevations along a centerline (with intermediate foresights) to plot the profile used for vertical-curve and grade design.

questions.question_text: True or False: In differential leveling, intermediate sights (other than turning points) are included in the ΣBS − ΣFS arithmetic check.
questions.explanation: Only the plus sights (BS) and the turning-point/closing minus sights (FS) carry the line of sight forward and enter ΣBS − ΣFS. Intermediate foresights (e.g., in profile leveling) give point elevations but are not part of the running check.

questions.question_text: A sight distance of 1,500 ft gives a curvature-only correction (C = 0.0240·F²) of most nearly:
Which statement about the HI method versus the rise-and-fall method of reducing levels is correct?
options: The HI method computes a line-of-sight elevation (HI) then subtracts foresights; the rise-and-fall method differences successive readings directly
The two methods give different final elevations
The HI method cannot use turning points
questions.correct_answer: The HI method computes a line-of-sight elevation (HI) then subtracts foresights; the rise-and-fall method differences successive readings directly
questions.explanation: Both yield identical elevations. The HI method (HI = Elev + BS, Elev = HI − FS) is faster for many intermediate sights; rise-and-fall provides an extra per-leg check.

problem_templates.name: Height of instrument from a benchmark
problem_templates.question_template: A level is set up and reads a backsight of {{bs:f2}} ft on benchmark BM-A, whose elevation is {{elevBM:f2}} ft. Compute the height of instrument (HI), in ft.
problem_templates.explanation_template: The backsight is a plus sight added to the known benchmark elevation: HI = {{elevBM:f2}} + {{bs:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Point elevation from HI and foresight
problem_templates.question_template: Benchmark BM-A has elevation {{elevBM:f2}} ft. The level reads a backsight of {{bs:f2}} ft on BM-A, then a foresight of {{fs:f2}} ft on point P. Compute the elevation of point P, in ft.
problem_templates.explanation_template: HI = {{elevBM:f2}} + {{bs:f2}} = {{hi:f2}} ft, then Elev_P = HI − FS = {{hi:f2}} − {{fs:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Multi-setup closing elevation (ΣBS − ΣFS)
problem_templates.question_template: A differential level run starts on BM-1 (elevation {{elevBM:f2}} ft). Over the run the sum of backsights is ΣBS = {{sumBS:f2}} ft and the sum of foresights is ΣFS = {{sumFS:f2}} ft. Compute the elevation of the closing point, in ft.
problem_templates.explanation_template: ΔElev = ΣBS − ΣFS = {{sumBS:f2}} − {{sumFS:f2}} = {{dElev:f2}} ft. Closing elevation = {{elevBM:f2}} + {{dElev:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Curvature & refraction correction
problem_templates.question_template: A level sight is {{distFt}} ft long. Using the combined curvature-and-refraction relation C_R = 0.0206·F² (F in thousands of feet), compute the correction to the rod reading, in ft.

solution_steps_template.title: Express distance in thousands of feet

problem_templates.explanation_template: With F = {{distFt}}/1000 = {{F:f2}} thousand ft, C_R = 0.0206·F² = {{_answer}} ft. This correction is subtracted from a long foresight; balancing BS and FS distances cancels it.

problem_templates.name: Trigonometric leveling — elevation of target
problem_templates.question_template: From station A (elevation {{elevA:f2}} ft) a total station measures a horizontal distance of {{dist:f1}} ft to a prism at a vertical angle of {{vAngle:dms}} above horizontal. Instrument and prism heights are equal. Compute the elevation of the prism point, in ft.

solution_steps_template.title: Result
solution_steps_template.result_template: {{_answer}} ft (calc: DEG mode, °’” key for the angle)

problem_templates.explanation_template: With equal instrument and prism heights, ΔElev = d·tan(V) = {{dist:f1}} × tan({{vAngle:dms}}) = {{dElev:f2}} ft, so the prism elevation = {{elevA:f2}} + {{dElev:f2}} = {{_answer}} ft.

problem_templates.name: Loop misclosure against a known benchmark
problem_templates.question_template: A level loop starts on BM-100 (elevation {{elevStart:f3}} ft) and is run to BM-101, whose published elevation is {{elevKnown:f3}} ft. The notes give ΣBS = {{sumBS:f3}} ft and ΣFS = {{sumFS:f3}} ft. Compute the loop misclosure (observed closing elevation − published elevation), in ft.

solution_steps_template.title: Result
solution_steps_template.result_template: {{_answer}} ft (distribute the opposite correction by distance/setups)

problem_templates.explanation_template: Observed closing elevation = {{elevStart:f3}} + ΣBS − ΣFS = {{obsClose:f3}} ft. Misclosure = observed − published = {{obsClose:f3}} − {{elevKnown:f3}} = {{_answer}} ft; the correction (opposite sign) is distributed in proportion to distance leveled.

mock_questions.question_text: A backsight of 4.78 ft is taken on a benchmark at elevation 521.36 ft; a foresight of 6.90 ft is then read on a turning point. The elevation of the turning point is most nearly:
In a differential level run, ΣBS = 27.65 ft and ΣFS = 24.10 ft. The closing point relative to the starting benchmark is:
mock_questions.explanation: ΔElev = ΣBS − ΣFS = 27.65 − 24.10 = +3.55 ft, so the closing point is 3.55 ft higher.

mock_questions.question_text: The combined curvature-and-refraction correction for a 5,000-ft sight (C_R = 0.0206·F², F in thousands of ft) is most nearly:

From a station at elevation 740.00 ft, a horizontal distance of 800.0 ft is measured to a target at a vertical angle of +2°30′ (equal instrument and target heights). The target elevation is most nearly:
Why are backsight and foresight distances kept approximately equal in differential leveling?
options: To cancel curvature, refraction, and collimation errors
To reduce the number of turning points
To make the rod readings smaller
To convert orthometric heights to ellipsoid heights
mock_questions.correct_answer: To cancel curvature, refraction, and collimation errors
mock_questions.explanation: Equal sight distances make the systematic errors equal on the BS and FS so they cancel in the difference.

mock_questions.question_text: Which field test is used to determine whether a level's line of sight is truly horizontal when the bubble is centered?
mock_questions.explanation: The two-peg test isolates collimation error by comparing an equal-distance setup with an unequal-distance setup.

mock_questions.question_text: The current official vertical datum used for orthometric elevations in the conterminous United States is:
mock_questions.explanation: NAVD88 is the current US vertical datum; NGVD29 is its predecessor; NAD83 and WGS84 are horizontal/3-D datums.

mock_questions.question_text: A level loop is run 2.0 miles and closes with a misclosure of 0.08 ft. If the third-order allowable closure is 0.05·√(miles), the loop:
mock_questions.explanation: Allowable = 0.05·√2.0 = 0.05 × 1.414 = 0.071 ft. The 0.08-ft misclosure exceeds this, so the loop fails third-order.

mock_questions.question_text: In three-wire leveling the rod reading used in the reduction is:
options: The mean of the upper, middle, and lower crosshair readings
The sum of all three readings
The upper minus the lower reading
mock_questions.correct_answer: The mean of the upper, middle, and lower crosshair readings
mock_questions.explanation: Reading = (U + M + L)/3, providing redundancy and a check (U − M ≈ M − L); the interval also yields the sight distance ≈ 100·(U − L).

mock_questions.question_text: A level note shows ΣBS − ΣFS = +12.43 ft and the starting and closing elevations differ by +12.40 ft. The +0.03-ft difference indicates:
options: An arithmetic or accumulated leveling error to be checked/distributed
mock_questions.correct_answer: An arithmetic or accumulated leveling error to be checked/distributed
mock_questions.explanation: ΣBS − ΣFS should equal the elevation difference exactly; a small residual is misclosure (accumulated error) to be verified and, if within tolerance, distributed.

mock_questions.question_text: Reciprocal leveling is most appropriate when:
options: A wide river or canyon prevents equal backsight and foresight distances
The line of sight is exactly horizontal
mock_questions.correct_answer: A wide river or canyon prevents equal backsight and foresight distances
mock_questions.explanation: When sights cannot be balanced, reading both points from each side and averaging cancels curvature, refraction, and collimation.

mock_questions.question_text: Leveling produces orthometric heights (H). To convert a GPS-derived ellipsoid height (h) to an orthometric height you apply the geoid height (N) using:
mock_questions.explanation: Orthometric height equals ellipsoid height minus geoid undulation: H = h − N.

mock_questions.question_text: A surveyor must establish the elevation of a point 0.6 mile from the nearest benchmark, with intermediate ground shots for a road profile. The most appropriate procedure is:
options: Differential and profile leveling carrying turning points from the benchmark
A single trigonometric sight with no benchmark tie
GPS RTK without a geoid model
mock_questions.correct_answer: Differential and profile leveling carrying turning points from the benchmark
mock_questions.explanation: Differential leveling carries control from the benchmark via turning points; profile leveling adds the intermediate ground shots for the road profile.

mock_questions.question_text: For a 3,000-ft sight, the earth-curvature-only correction (C = 0.0240·F², F in thousands of ft) is most nearly:
mock_questions.explanation: F = 3; C = 0.0240 × 9 = 0.216 ft. (Combined with refraction it would be 0.0206 × 9 = 0.185 ft.)
